1.blurb: In Hellenistic Alexandria around 270 BCE, the Greek astronomer Aristarchus of Samos had already proposed that the Earth orbits the Sun — eighteen centuries before Copernicus, and with no way to prove it. It was the right answer arriving two millennia early, and it was set aside, a reminder that being correct is not enough without the evidence and the instruments to compel assent.

In the Northern Song, the polymath Shen Kuo tested claims by direct observation, recognized fossil seashells in mountains as old seabeds, and described magnetic declination in 1088 — an empirical, authority-doubting temper centuries before Bacon. The Needham Question (Chapter 20) asks why this did not become a self-sustaining scientific revolution there.

Founded in Mongol Persia in 1259, the Maragha observatory was a state-funded, staffed, instrument-equipped research institution whose astronomers built the Tusi couple, the exact geometric device that reappears in Copernicus three centuries later. Whether the knowledge travelled or was reinvented is one of the live questions of this whole story.
The Scientific Revolution grew straight out of the late Renaissance — the same humanist recovery of ancient texts, now turned on nature instead of rhetoric.
Song China had movable type, gunpowder, the compass, advanced mathematics — and no Scientific Revolution. The puzzle of why Europe and not Song China runs through this whole story.
The world the revolution overthrew was Aristotle's — Greek natural philosophy, transmitted for two thousand years as settled authority.
Ptolemy's Roman-era astronomy was the system Copernicus dethroned — the cosmos as the classical world had left it.

3.blurb: The ninth-century Baghdad astronomer al-Battani measured the length of the year to within two minutes of the modern value. Copernicus cited him by name dozens of times, and Tycho Brahe used his observations as the baseline for his own — the line from al-Battani to the new astronomy is direct, not a European cold start.
Tycho's data-obsessed naked-eye astronomy was being matched, unconnected, by late-Ming and earlier Chinese and Timurid observatories — accurate observation was not uniquely European.
Ulugh Beg's Samarkand observatory had produced star tables of Tycho-like precision two centuries earlier — the European breakthrough was what came after the data, not the data itself.

5.blurb: Around 1011 in Cairo, Ibn al-Haytham wrote the Book of Optics, proving vision works by light entering the eye and pioneering controlled experiment to settle the question. The European route to the telescope, and to Kepler's and Newton's optics, ran through his work — the lens was standing on Cairo's shoulders.
Galileo worked inside the Italian city-state world the Renaissance built — Medici patronage and all, which is also what made his trial possible.

7.blurb: Around 1020 the Persian philosopher Ibn Sina ran his floating man thought experiment: a sensationless, suspended mind would still have to affirm its own existence. Descartes reached the structurally identical point — I think, therefore I am — six centuries later, an arrival the reference chain records as independent but not unprecedented.

8.blurb: Around 1242 in Cairo, the physician Ibn al-Nafis denied Galen's porous heart-wall and correctly described the passage of blood through the lungs — pulmonary circulation, three centuries before Harvey. His Arabic text lay unread in Europe until 1924, so Harvey owed him nothing: the same truth, found twice, a civilization apart.

9.blurb: The engineer al-Jazari, working in the Artuqid court around 1206, built a double-action suction pump whose design implied a working command of vacuum and atmospheric pressure that European theory would not articulate until Torricelli and Pascal four centuries later.

11.blurb: The Mouseion and Library of Alexandria, founded under the Ptolemies around 295 BCE, housed and funded scholars to pursue knowledge — in effect the world's first state-funded research institution. It is the deepest precedent for the scientific society, and also the thing the Royal Society differs from: Alexandria died with its dynasty; the Society was built to outlive any patron.
The 'Republic of Letters' consciously revived the Hellenistic ideal of an international learned community — Alexandria's library reborn as a continent-wide correspondence network.
Its model was the humanist letter-network the Italian Renaissance had pioneered, now scaled up and turned to natural knowledge.

13.blurb: The mechanical, mathematized worldview built here is the direct intellectual ancestor of the Industrial Revolution's confidence that nature is an engine to be harnessed.

19.blurb: What the revolution left out — and what it enabled — flows directly into the Enlightenment's project of applying the new method to society itself.

20.blurb: The Song dynasty is the civilization economic historians return to as the closest pre-modern analog to an industrial society, and its own chapter runs the debate — incomplete markets, missing institutions, Mongol disruption, scholar-gentry priorities — over why it did not spark an industrial revolution six centuries before Britain. That is the Needham Question, argued from the other side.
The 'Why Europe?' question only sharpens against Song China: more advanced in 1100, it produced no Principia. The answer lies in institutions and competition, not raw cleverness.
